content.full_text:
State of California

Memorandum

To  : Opinion Request Meeting Participants          Date  : Oct. 15, 1981


From : FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION              A-81-10-077
       BOB L.

Subject: Legally Required Participation


Background

     A redevelopment agency has been created.  Under redevelopment
law (H&S Code §33000et seq.), the executive power (mayor) may
appoint a five-member body to function as the agency (H&S Code
§33110), or the legislative body may adopt an ordinance making
themselves that body (H&S Code §33200).  If the legislative body
elects to do that, it may still create a community redevelopment
commission (H&S Code §33201) which would then develop and adopt
the redevelopment plan (H&S Code §33202).  The legislative body
may also change its mind and step down as the redevelopment agency
and direct the executive (mayor) to appoint a five or seven-member
agency board (H&S Code §33203).  In addition, the legislative
body, while functioning as a redevelopment agency may delegate
any or all of its functions or powers to a community (H&S Code
§33205).

Issue

     Where a five-member city council has elected to function as
the redevelopment agency and 3 or 4 members have conflicts of
interest which bar them from participation in many basic redevelopment
decisions, does the "legally required participation" exception
apply, permitting some of them to participate?  Or, do the provisions
outlined above constitute an "alternative source of decision
consistent with the purposes and terms of the statute authorizing
the decision" (2 Cal. Adm. Code §18701(a))?

BL:plh
Attachments

ANSWER

     The resolution to this issue was as follows:  We would not
conclude that the statutory alternatives constituted an alternative
source of decision-making under 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18701(a)
where no alternative decision-making body actually was in existence.

                                                          18701(a)

sections.question: Where a five-member city council has elected to function as the redevelopment agency and 3 or 4 members have conflicts of interest which bar them from participation in many basic redevelopment decisions, does the "legally required participation" exception apply, permitting some of them to participate? Or, do the provisions outlined above constitute an "alternative source of decision consistent with the purposes and terms of the statute authorizing the decision" (2 Cal. Adm. Code §18701(a))?
sections.conclusion: The resolution to this issue was as follows: We would not conclude that the statutory alternatives constituted an alternative source of decision-making under 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18701(a) where no alternative decision-making body actually was in existence.
sections.facts:
A redevelopment agency has been created. Under redevelopment law (H&S Code §33000 et seq.), the executive power (mayor) may appoint a five-member body to function as the agency (H&S Code §33110), or the legislative body may adopt an ordinance making themselves that body (H&S Code §33200). If the legislative body elects to do that, it may still create a community redevelopment commission (H&S Code §33201) which would then develop and adopt the redevelopment plan (H&S Code §33202). The legislative body may also change its mind and step down as the redevelopment agency and direct the executive (mayor) to appoint a five or seven-member agency board (H&S Code §33203). In addition, the legislative body, while functioning as a redevelopment agency may delegate any or all of its functions or powers to a community (H&S Code §33205).

Issue

Where a five-member city council has elected to function as the redevelopment agency and 3 or 4 members have conflicts of interest which bar them from participation in many basic redevelopment decisions, does the "legally required participation" exception apply, permitting some of them to participate? Or, do the provisions outlined above constitute an "alternative source of decision consistent with the purposes and terms of the statute authorizing the decision" (2 Cal. Adm. Code §18701(a))?

BL:plh
Attachments

ANSWER

The resolution to this issue was as follows: We would not conclude that the statutory alternatives constituted an alternative source of decision-making under 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18701(a) where no alternative decision-making body actually was in existence.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether city council members with conflicts of interest in redevelopment decisions can invoke the "legally required participation" exception or whether statutory alternatives to the redevelopment agency structure constitute an alternative decision-making source under Political Reform Act regulations.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC concluded that statutory alternatives to the redevelopment agency structure do not constitute an alternative source of decision-making under the regulation when no such alternative body actually exists in practice.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: old; LLM: Internal memorandum format rather than standard advice letter format. Question and Answer sections clearly identifiable. Background section provides extensive statutory context on redevelopment agency structure. Document appears to be from internal FPPC meeting materials discussing opinion request resolution.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Where a five-member city council has elected to function as the redevelopment agency and 3 or 4 members have conflicts of interest which bar them from participation in many basic redevelopment decisions, does the "legally required participation" exception apply, permitting some of them to participate? Or, do the provisions outlined above constitute an "alternative source of decision consistent with the purposes and terms of the statute authorizing the decision" (2 Cal. Adm. Code §18701(a))?

CONCLUSION: The resolution to this issue was as follows: We would not conclude that the statutory alternatives constituted an alternative source of decision-making under 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18701(a) where no alternative decision-making body actually was in existence.

Under redevelopment law (H&S Code §33000et seq.), the executive power (mayor) may appoint a five-member body to function as the agency (H&S Code §33110), or the legislative body may adopt an ordinance making themselves that body (H&S Code §33200). If the legislative body elects to do that, it may still create a community redevelopment commission (H&S Code §33201) which would then develop and adopt the redevelopment plan (H&S Code §33202). The legislative body may also change its mind and step down as the redevelopment agency and direct the executive (mayor) to appoint a five or seven-member agency board (H&S Code §33203). Code Section 18701(a) where no alternative decision-making body actually was in existence. 18701(a)
embedding.summary: An internal FPPC memorandum analyzing whether city council members functioning as a redevelopment agency with conflicts of interest can rely on the "legally required participation" exception or statutory alternatives under the Political Reform Act. The FPPC concluded that statutory alternatives do not qualify as alternative decision-making sources when they have not been actually established.
